skill.name: target-novelty-scorer
skill.description: Score the novelty of biological targets based on literature mining from PubMed and other academic databases, assessing research popularity, uniqueness, and innovation potential.
skill.summary: A bioinformatics tool that scores biological target novelty from 0 to 100 using multi-dimensional literature analysis.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 69 potential issues, but 66 of 69 are false positives. The scanner confused markdown backtick formatting (inline code and fenced code blocks) with Ruby shell backtick execution across 37 locations in documentation files. All 19 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags are false positives triggered by score threshold comparisons and argument parser configuration. The 3 confirmed findings are legitimate functional risks: network access to NCBI E-utilities API (essential for PubMed literature mining), file system write operations for report output, and an unused API key parameter in the class constructor. No prompt injection, obfuscation, or malicious intent detected.

medium_findings.title: Network Access to External API
medium_findings.description: Code contains a hardcoded URL to NCBI E-utilities API (https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils) for PubMed literature retrieval. This is legitimate for a bioinformatics literature mining tool but constitutes external network access outside the sandbox. The current implementation uses simulated data and does not make live API calls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Hardcoded NCBI URL confirmed in source code at line 38. URL points to standard NCBI E-utilities endpoint. Code currently uses simulated data, so no live network calls execute. Pattern is legitimate for the stated purpose.

medium_findings.title: File System Write Without Path Sanitization
medium_findings.description: Code writes to a user-specified output file path via open() with write mode. The path comes directly from the --output argument with no path traversal validation, allowing arbitrary file writes if the argument is not controlled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: File write operation confirmed at line 446 with open(args.output, 'w'). No path traversal or directory restriction checks present. Risk is mitigated by CLI argument-based invocation in sandboxed environments.

medium_findings.title: API Key Parameter With No Environment Variable Source
medium_findings.description: The PubMedSearcher class constructor accepts an optional api_key parameter. While no os.environ or getenv calls exist currently, the class is structured to receive API keys, which could expose credentials if integrated with environment variables in the future.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Constructor parameter api_key confirmed at lines 36-37 with Optional[str] type. No actual environment variable access exists. This is a design pattern concern, not an active credential leak.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Formatting Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 37 instances of markdown backtick usage in SKILL.md and references/audit-reference.md as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. All instances are markdown formatting for inline code, code blocks, or documentation references. No actual shell execution backticks exist in these files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All 37 flagged locations are confirmed as markdown syntax (inline `code` and fenced ``` code blocks ```), not Ruby backtick shell execution. These are false positives.

low_findings.title: Dict .keys() Method Flagged as Certificate File Access
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged year_dist.keys() at scripts/main.py:197 as 'Certificate/key files'. The .keys() call is a standard Python dictionary method returning view of dictionary keys, not accessing certificate files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed: line 197 contains years = sorted(year_dist.keys()), a standard Python dictionary method call. Not certificate-related.

low_findings.title: Score Comparators Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 15 instances of score threshold comparisons (e.g., 'if score >= 80') and argument parser configuration as 'Weak cryptographic algorithm'. These are novelty score interpretation thresholds and argparse setup, not cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged locations confirmed as non-cryptographic code: score comparison logic, argparse configuration, markdown headers, YAML frontmatter, and JSON data. No encryption or hashing operations present.

low_findings.title: Documentation Text Flagged as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged markdown documentation about usage parameters, audit commands, and evaluation criteria as 'System reconnaissance'. These are descriptive text sections in SKILL.md and JSON data in audit result files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Flagged lines are documentation describing usage parameters, audit commands, and test case scenarios. No actual system reconnaissance code exists.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Network API Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Scripts/main.py contains a hardcoded URL to the NCBI E-utilities API endpoint for PubMed data retrieval. While currently using simulated data, the class is designed to make external HTTP requests to this endpoint when integrated with a real API key.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: Script writes output to a file path specified by the --output command-line argument without path traversal validation, enabling write to arbitrary filesystem locations if the argument is not properly restricted.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key Credential Parameter
dangerous_patterns.description: The PubMedSearcher constructor accepts an API key as a parameter. Though currently unused in the simulated implementation, the class is structurally designed to receive and potentially transmit credentials to external APIs.

content.user_title: Score Target Novelty Through Literature Mining
content.value_statement: Researchers struggle to assess how novel a biological target is for drug discovery. This skill scores target novelty from 0 to 100 by analyzing research heat, uniqueness, research depth, collaboration networks, and temporal trends from literature data.
actual_capabilities: Scores biological target novelty on a 0 to 100 scale using multi-dimensional analysis across five criteria
Analyzes research heat, uniqueness, research depth, collaboration network diversity, and publication temporal trends
Calculates confidence levels based on total literature volume to indicate result reliability
Generates structured novelty reports in text, JSON, or CSV output formats for flexible downstream use
Provides plain-language novelty level interpretations from Very Low to Extremely High with supporting statistics
Supports configurable analysis year range and data source selection for customized research scope
limitations: Uses simulated literature data by default instead of live PubMed API calls for demonstration purposes
Requires an NCBI API key for actual PubMed data retrieval when moving beyond simulated results
Scoring weights are hardcoded in the script and cannot be adjusted through command-line arguments
Analyzes one target at a time and does not support batch comparison of multiple targets in a single run

use_cases.title: Drug Target Discovery Screening
use_cases.description: A drug discovery researcher evaluates the novelty of multiple potential biological targets to prioritize which targets warrant deeper investigation and resource allocation.

use_cases.title: Academic Research Direction Analysis
use_cases.description: An academic scientist assesses the novelty of their proposed research target to identify underexplored directions with high innovation potential for grant applications.

use_cases.title: Research Portfolio Novelty Assessment
use_cases.description: A research strategist evaluates the novelty distribution across their organization's target portfolio to identify gaps, overlaps, and strategic opportunities.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Novelty Check for a Single Target
prompt_templates.prompt: Score the novelty of target EGFR using the target-novelty-scorer skill with default settings.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting a fast novelty assessment for a well-known biological target

prompt_templates.title: Detailed Trend Analysis Over Extended Period
prompt_templates.prompt: Run target-novelty-scorer on target BRCA1 with a 10-year analysis window and verbose output enabled to examine long-term research trends.
prompt_templates.scenario: Analyzing research trajectory and growth patterns for a specific gene target

prompt_templates.title: JSON Report Generation for Data Pipelines
prompt_templates.prompt: Use target-novelty-scorer to analyze target PD-L1 and output the results in JSON format for integration with downstream data processing tools.
prompt_templates.scenario: Generating machine-readable output for automated analysis pipelines

prompt_templates.title: Cross-Database Comparison With Custom Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Run target-novelty-scorer with --target KRAS --db all --years 5 --output kras_novelty.json --format json to produce a structured comparison report across multiple data sources.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analysis using all available data sources with custom output path

output_examples.input: Score novelty of target BRCA1 with default 5-year analysis window.
output_examples.output: The skill generates a text report showing an overall novelty score of 42.3/100 with moderate confidence across five dimensions: research heat, uniqueness, research depth, collaboration network, and temporal trend. The interpretation labels this as Low Novelty with supporting statistics on total papers, recent publications, and clinical trials.

output_examples.input: Analyze target PD-L1 with JSON output format.
output_examples.output: A structured JSON report containing the target name, novelty score (72.5), confidence level (0.85), dimension breakdown scores, metadata including total papers (15234) and clinical trials (89), and a text interpretation summarizing the novelty assessment.

best_practices: Validate target names against official gene symbols before analysis to ensure accurate and meaningful results.
Use JSON output format when you need to process results programmatically or integrate with other bioinformatics tools.
Adjust the analysis year range to match your specific research domain, as different fields have different publication cycles.
anti_patterns: Analyzing targets without specifying the correct gene symbol leads to misleading scores that do not reflect the actual research landscape.
Ignoring confidence scores when interpreting novelty can result in overconfident decisions about early-stage targets with limited literature.
Using default settings without adjusting for your specific research domain limits the relevance and accuracy of the novelty assessment.

faq.question: How does the novelty scoring algorithm work?
faq.answer: The algorithm scores targets from 0 to 100 using five weighted dimensions: research heat (25%), uniqueness (25%), research depth (20%), collaboration network (15%), and temporal trend (15%). Each dimension is scored based on literature volume, citation patterns, clinical trial data, and publication growth rates.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to real PubMed data?
faq.answer: The current implementation uses simulated data for demonstration. The code includes a hardcoded NCBI E-utilities API URL and is designed for real PubMed integration, but you need to provide an NCBI API key and modify the search method to make live API calls.

faq.question: What output formats are available?
faq.answer: Three output formats are supported: text (human-readable formatted report), JSON (structured data for programmatic processing), and CSV (tabular format for spreadsheet import). Use the --format argument to select your preferred format.

faq.question: How should I interpret the confidence score?
faq.answer: Confidence scores range from 0.4 to 0.9 based on the volume of available literature. Scores above 0.85 indicate robust data support (over 1000 papers), while scores below 0.6 suggest limited literature and require cautious interpretation.

faq.question: Can I analyze multiple targets in a single run?
faq.answer: No, this skill analyzes one target at a time per invocation. You need to run the skill separately for each target you want to evaluate. Batch comparison is not currently supported.

faq.question: Do I need an API key to use this skill?
faq.answer: No API key is required for the default simulated mode. If you want real PubMed data, you need an NCBI API key, which can be obtained for free from the NCBI website. The skill accepts the API key through the PubMedSearcher class constructor.
